items.title: Fraud detection at a bank
items.scenario: A classifier flags 1.2% of card transactions as fraud. On a labeled audit set: TP=240, FP=60, FN=160, TN=49,540. Precision = 240/300 = 0.80; recall = 240/400 = 0.60. Investigators accept moderate recall because each false alarm costs analyst time, but they track recall weekly.
items.explanation: Precision answers 'are alerts real fraud?'; recall answers 'what fraction of fraud did we stop?'. The team may lower the threshold if recall drops below a policy floor.

items.title: Medical screening for a rare condition
items.scenario: A screening model on 10,000 patients finds 8 true cases (TP=8) but misses 4 (FN=4). It raises 42 false alarms (FP=42). Recall = 8/12 ≈ 0.67; precision = 8/50 = 0.16. Clinicians prioritize recall—missing disease is harmful—so they accept low precision and follow up all positives.
items.explanation: Rare positives make precision naturally low at high-recall operating points; stakeholders must agree which error is worse.

items.title: Email spam filter tuning
items.scenario: Product sets threshold so precision ≥ 0.99 (almost no good mail in spam folder). Recall lands at 0.85—15% of spam reaches the inbox. F1 = 0.92. Marketing wants higher recall; they slide threshold until precision hits 0.995 and measure recall drop.
items.explanation: Threshold tuning is an explicit precision–recall negotiation, not a single 'best' model setting.

items.title: Multiclass intent detection in a chatbot
items.scenario: A four-class intent model reports macro-F1 = 0.74 and micro-F1 = 0.81. Macro-F1 exposes that the rare 'billing dispute' class scores F1 = 0.52 while 'greeting' scores 0.95. The team improves dispute examples rather than trusting micro-F1 alone.
items.explanation: Macro averaging surfaces weak rare classes; micro averaging mirrors overall token volume and can hide failures on minorities.
